coredata.dc:title: Innovative approach for the electrochemical detection of non-electroactive organophosphorus pesticides using oxime as electroactive probe
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Jing
Hou, Juying
Jiang, Jianxia
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: An innovative approach for sensitive and simple electrochemical detection of non-electroactive organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) was described in this report. The novel strategy emphasized the fabrication of an oxime-based sensor via attaching pralidoxime (PAM) on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified glassy carbon electrode. The introduction of GQDs significantly increased the effective electrode area, and then enlarged the immobilization quantity of PAM. Thus, the oxidation current of PAM was obviously increased. Relying on the nucleophilic substitution reaction between oxime and OPs, fenthion was detected using PAM as the electroactive probe. Under optimum conditions, the difference of oxidation current of PAM was proportional to fenthion concentration over the range from 1.0×10−11 M to 5.0×10−7 M with a detection limit of 6.8×10−12 M (S/N=3). Moreover, the favorable detection performance in water and soil samples heralded the promising applications in on-site OPs detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical detection
Pralidoxime
Organophosphorus pesticides
Electroactive probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural characterization of graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fabrication process and the detection principle of the sensor.
TEM image (A) and the size distribution of GQDs (B), XPS C1s analysis of GO (C) and QDs (D).
Cyclic voltammograms of the bare GCE (a), GCE/PAM (b), GCE/GQDs (c), GCE/GQDs/PAM (d) and GCE/GQDs/PAM treated with fenthion (e) in 0.1M acetate buffer (0.1M, pH 5.5). Scan rate, 100mVs−1.

(A) DPV responses of GCE/GQDs/PAM reacted with different concentration of fenthion. (a–k: 0, 1×10−11, 5×10−11, 1×10−10, 5×10−10, 1×10−9, 5×10−9, 1×10−8, 5×10−8, 1×10−7, 5×10−7 M). (B) Calibration plot for detecting fenthion.
Recovery studies of fenthion in spiked river water and soil samples (n =3).

head.ce:title: Innovative approach for the electrochemical detection of non-electroactive organophosphorus pesticides using oxime as electroactive probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative approach for sensitive and simple electrochemical detection of non-electroactive organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) was described in this report. The novel strategy emphasized the fabrication of an oxime-based sensor via attaching pralidoxime (PAM) on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified glassy carbon electrode. The introduction of GQDs significantly increased the effective electrode area, and then enlarged the immobilization quantity of PAM. Thus, the oxidation current of PAM was obviously increased. Relying on the nucleophilic substitution reaction between oxime and OPs, fenthion was detected using PAM as the electroactive probe. Under optimum conditions, the difference of oxidation current of PAM was proportional to fenthion concentration over the range from 1.0×10−11 M to 5.0×10−7 M with a detection limit of 6.8×10−12 M (S/N=3). Moreover, the favorable detection performance in water and soil samples heralded the promising applications in on-site OPs detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate pesticides (OPs) are the most widely used pesticides in agriculture due to their high effectiveness for insect eradication and relatively low persistence under natural conditions [1]. However, because of the ability of irreversible inhibition of acetylcholineesterase (AChE) and bioaccumulation effects in human body, OPs exhibit acute toxicity to human health through their residues in agricultural products and contaminated water [2]. Therefore, accurate and sensitive detection methods are in urgent demand for the screening of OPs residues. Conventional analytical methods for OPs detection such as gas chromatography, mass spectroscopy, liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (LC/MS) or gas chromatography (GC/MS) [3–5] are still considered to be the most reliable techniques owing to their high selectivity and adequate sensitivity. However, these laboratory-based chromatographic analytical techniques are underdetermined by their disadvantages of time-consuming, complicated sample pretreatment, requirements for sophisticated instrument and skilled professional, which limits their applications for on-site detection in most settings, especially in emergency cases. As a result, portable sensors that can be applied for on-site rapid detection are of great practical interest.

In the past few decades, electrochemical methods were emerged as the most promising alternative technique for on-site detection of OPs due to their inherent virtues such as low cost, miniaturization and automation, simple preparation and high sensitivity [6]. Enzyme/antibody-based electrochemical biosensors have been found extensive used in OPs detection. Although high sensitivity and specificity can be achieved, the poor chemical/physical stability of the enzymes and antibodies prevents their use in harsh environments of acids, bases or organic solvents, and high temperature [7]. To meet the requirements of rapid warning and field deployment, much attention has been given to direct electrochemical sensing of OPs in recent years. In general, these analyses utilize the electrochemical redox of analyte as detection signals, which is only suitable for sensing of OPs with inherent redox activity such as methyl parathion, paraoxon and fenitrothion [8,9]. How can other OPs without electrochemical redox activity be detected using similar methods? It is still a great challenge for numerous researchers.

Some approaches for OPs assay based on oxime compounds have aroused much interest recently. Oximes, such as pralidoxime (PAM), have been utilized as effective antidotes for OP compounds. As a kind of super nucleophile, oxime can reversibly bind to OP-inactivated AChE then attach to the free OPs, efficiently reversing and preventing other irreversible binding of OPs to AChE [6]. Taking advantage of the good reactivity between oximes and OPs, several oxime-based sensors have been developed by means of colorimetric [10], fluorescent [11], and chemiluminescent [12] methods. Surprisingly, little attention has been given to oxime-based electrochemical assays, despite of the simplicity, promptness and compact nature of electrochemical instrument. Herein, we proposed a novel strategy for non-electroactive OPs sensing relying on the nucleophilic substitution reaction between oxime and OPs. The strategy involved constructing a graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and attaching PAM, a common used antidotes for OPs, on GQDs via electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction. Using the electrochemical oxidation of PAM as detection signal, fenthion, a chosen model of non-electroactive organophosphorus, was quantified through measuring the change in oxidation current of PAM before and after reaction with target analyte. It is anticipated that this novel method will open new opportunities for fast, simple, and sensitive analysis of OPs in environmental and biological samples.

Fenthion (O,O-dimethyl-O-(4-methylthio-m-tolyl)phosphorothioate) was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA), and its stock solution was prepared in anhydrous ethanol. Graphite powder, chitosan and pralidoxime iodide (pyridine-2-aldoxime methoiodide, PAM) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixing stock solutions of 0.1M NaH2PO4 and 0.1M Na2HPO4 and adjusting the pH with 0.1M NaOH or 0.1M HCl. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and all the aqueous solutions were prepared with redistilled water.

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI660C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Co., China). A conventional three-electrode system was used, comprising a bare or modified GCE as working electrode, a platinum wire as auxiliary electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode. All electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature (25±2°C). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were obtained using the JEOL-1200EX TEM (Japan). X-rays photoeletron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a VG ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using a nonmonochromatized Al Kα X-ray source.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized according to the following steps [13]. Firstly, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummer’s method [14] (detailed in Supplementary data). The synthesized GO was dispersed in water to obtain a yellow-brown aqueous solution with the aid of ultrasonication. Subsequently, GO solution (30mL, 0.5mg/mL) was mixed carefully with concentrated HNO3 (8mL) and H2SO4 (2mL). Then the mixture was heated and refluxed under microwave irradiation for 2h in a microwave oven operating at a power of 240W. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was kept under mild ultrasonication for a few minutes, and the pH was tuned to 8 with NaOH in an ice-bath. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the large tracts of GO and a deep yellow solution was separated. At last, the mixture solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 8000–10,000Da) and dried for further use.

A schematic graph of the fabrication process is shown in Scheme 1 . Prior to modification, the bare GCE was polished to a mirror-like surface with 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurry on micro-cloth pads and then thoroughly cleaned before use. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized according to Li’s method with some modification [13]. Subsequently, 5μL of GQDs suspension (2.0mg/mL in 0.05% (w/v) chitosan solution) was dropped onto the surface of GCE. To save time, the electrode was dried under an infrared lamp and was denoted as GCE/GQDs. Then the GCE/GQDs was immersed in a stirred aqueous solution containing 0.5mM of PAM at 0.5V for 150s forming GCE/GQDs/PAM. In parallel, GCE/PAM was prepared as control.

Water samples were obtained from Naihe River located in the Taian region in Shandong (China). Before use, water samples were filtered with a 0.45μm filter to eliminate particulate matters and the pH was adjusted to 7.5. These samples were then probed with a known concentration of fenthion and stored in refrigerator (4°C) for use.

Soil samples were collected from trial plots of Shandong Agricultural University (China). Before use, soils were homogenized, sieved (2-mm mesh) and air-dried at room temperature. Spiking was performed as follows: Ten grams of homogenized soil was mixed with appropriate amounts of fenthion-spiked solutions prepared in ethanol. After 24h, they were extracted with 50mL of ethanol in an ultrasonic bath for 30min. Afterwards, the samples were centrifuged at 4000rpm for 10min and the supernatant was separated. At last, the soil extracts were diluted with 0.1M PBS (pH 7.5) and were assayed as described above.

For electrochemical measurement of pesticides, the GCE/GQDs/PAM was firstly immersed in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.5) containing different concentrations of standard pesticides or fenthion-spiked real samples (water or soil samples) for 15min. After washing with distilled water, the electrode was immediately transferred into an acetate buffer (ABS, 0.1M, pH 5.5) for differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measure. The DPV was carried out from +0.55 to +0.90V with the parameters of increment potential 4mV, pulse amplitude 50mV, pulse width 0.05 s and sample width 0.0167s. The current change (ΔI) of PAM was calculated as follows:
Δ I =
I
0
−
I i
where I 0 and Ii were the electrochemical oxidation current of PAM before and after reaction with pesticides.

The fabrication strategy of this new sensor for detection of OPs is illustrated in Scheme 1. The construction process consists of two steps: (1) immobilizing GQDs onto the surface of GCE and (2) attachment of PAM on GQDs modified GCE. By means of the favorable film-forming ability of chitosan, GQDs were uniformly immobilized on GCE. The net charges of carboxyl group on GQDs appear to be negative while the nitrogen atom on the pyridine ring of PAM appears to be positive [15,16], thus plenty of PAM molecules can be adsorbed on GQDs film due to electrostatic attraction. In addition, the π–π stacking interaction between GQDs and pyridine rings will increase the adsorption capacity and stability of immobilized PAM. PAM molecule can be electrochemically oxidized at anode and give an oxidation peak. After undergoing nucleophilic substitution reaction with OPs, the oxidation current of PAM will decrease. As the reduction value is in proportion with the quantity of OPs that has been reacted, the current change of PAM prior to and after reaction can be used to assess the concentration of OPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows typical TEM image and XPS spectra of the synthesized GQDs. TEM showed that the collected GQDs were monodispersing (Fig. 1A) and had a uniform diameter of ca. 2–7nm, with the average size of 4.2nm (Fig. 1B). XPS measurements were carried out to probe the composition of as-prepared GQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 1D, the XPS of GQDs showed three kinds of carbon: graphitic carbon (CC and CC), oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon. The spectrum of C1s confirmed the presence of CC (284.3eV), CO (286.9eV) and CO (287.8eV) bonds, indicating the as-prepared GQDs were rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surfaces [17]. The CN bond (285.8eV) verified the presence of nitrogen in GQDs. The doped nitrogen was originated from the treatment of HNO3 in the preparation process of GQDs [13]. The presence of O-rich groups makes the GQDs soluble in aqueous medium and provides additional advantages for further functionalization meeting various requirements. Obviously, the C-O peak intensity was lower than that of GO (seen from Fig. 1C), suggesting that reduction reaction took place during the microwave treatment for GQD preparation.

Cyclic voltammograms obtained at pH 5.5 on GCE electrode before and after each step of modification are presented in Fig. 2 . Among these modified electrodes, there were no redox peaks at bare GCE (curve a) and GCE/GQDs (curve c), indicating the non-electroactive character of GQDs. However, the background current for the GCE/GQDs was much larger than that for bare GCE, which mainly result from the increased effective electrode area. After modified with GQDs, the effective area increased about 5 times (detailed in Supplementary data). An irreversible oxidation peak was observed at the GCE/PAM (curve b) and GCE/GQDs/PAM (curve d). The oxidation peak corresponded to the electrochemical oxidation of the aldoxime group of PAM to carbonyl group via a two-electron-transfer mechanism, which has been detailed discussed by previous literatures [15,16]. Compared with bare GCE, the oxidation potential of PAM at GCE/GQDs/PAM negatively shifted from +0.76V to +0.71V, and the oxidation current enhanced significantly. These results should ascribe to the contribution of GQDs, which remarkably increased the effective electrode area, thus could augment the electrode reaction sites, enhance the adsorption capacity and amplify the current response of PAM. As expected, the oxidation current for GCE/GQDs/PAM markedly decreased (curve e) after being treated with fenthion. This is because partial PAM on electrode surface reacted with fenthion (presented in Eq. (1)), leading to the reduced oxidation current.

In order to achieve optimal performance, various experimental parameters affecting the sensitivity of the sensor such as accumulation potential and accumulation time were optimized (detailed in Supplementary data). Under optimum conditions, DPV was used for the quantitative analysis of fenthion within the potential range from +0.55 to +0.90V. The calibration curve was performed using different GCE/GQDs/PAM for the quantification. Each modified electrode only works once. After a determination, the electrode was polished to a mirror-like surface with 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurry on micro-cloth pads and then thoroughly cleaned. Then the cleaned bare electrode was modified with GQDs and PAM for next determination. Fig. 3 A presented DPV curves of the GCE/GQDs/PAM after reaction with different concentrations of fenthion. It was evident that a well-defined DPV curve from electrochemical oxidation of PAM was observed (curve a), and it gradually decreased with the increase of the fenthion concentration (curves b–k). This is because the nucleophilic substitution reaction can decrease the concentration of PAM molecules, resulting in the lower oxidation current. Good linear relationship between current change of PAM (ΔI) and log C (fenthion) was obtained from 1×10−11 M to 5×10−7 M with the regression equation of ΔI (μA)=16.40+1.44×log C (M) (R 2 =0.996) (Fig. 3B). A detection limit of 6.8×10−12 M estimated from signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The good performance of the proposed method should attribute to the excellent adsorption capacity of GQDs and the sensitive electrochemical oxidation of PAM. The main analytical characteristics of the proposed method were compared with other previously studied techniques for determination of fenthion and the results were summarized in Table S1 (in Supplementary data). It can be seen that the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the proposed method are similar to other methods. The analytical range is wider than that of the most of the other methods. Limit of detection (LOD) is better than some and comparable with those of the other studies. Therefore, the proposed method can be used for sensitive OPs determination.

To further demonstrate the practicality of the proposed method, the recovery test was carried out by processing a series of river water and soil samples. Standard addition method was adopted to estimate the practicality. The recoveries in real samples of river water and soil samples [18] were investigated and the data were summarized in Table 1 . The recoveries of the spiked fenthion were from 94.6% to 105.3% (as shown in Table 1), suggesting that this new method was highly accurate and can be used for assay of real samples.

The inter-assay precision was estimated by investigating the electrochemical oxidation responses of six different GCE/GQDs/PAM electrodes in 0.1M ABS (pH 5.5) after dipping in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.5) containing 5×10−9 M fenthion for 15min. Similarly, the intra-assay precision was evaluated by six replicate determinations using one working electrode under uniform conditions. The relative standard deviation values of inter-assay and intra-assay were found to be 4.6% and 3.8%, respectively, indicating acceptable precision and reproducibility. In addition, the prepared GCE/GQDs/PAM was very stable at room temperature. No obvious decrease in the electrochemistry response was observed in the first week and nearly 90% of the initial response remained after one month, demonstrating acceptable stability.

Possible interfering substances that coexist with OPs in water and soil samples such as inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2− and Cl−), saccharides (sucrose and fructose) and other electroactive organic compounds (p-nitrophenol and benzenediol) were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor. The results demonstrated that 104-fold of inorganic ions, 103-fold of saccharides and 102-fold of phenols had no obvious influence on the signal of PAM. These results showed that there was little interference from commonly existed substances, demonstrating that the sensor was suitable for analytical application.

Herein, we fabricated a novel oxime-based electrochemical sensor for non-electroactive OPs determination by using PAM as the electroactive probe. The detective performance was dramatically enhanced by means of the QDs as fixation matrix for PAM, which can increase the electrode reaction site, enhance the adsorption capacity, amplify the current response of PAM therefore, improves the determination sensitivity. The resulting sensor displayed high sensitivity, good reproducibility, and favorable performance in real samples sensing. We believe that the new strategy will pave the way to important applications in on-site pesticides detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21375079) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong province (No. ZR2014BM008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.05.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials for sensing and destroying pesticides
The vital function of Fe3O4@Au nanocomposites for hydrolase biosensor design and its application in detection of methyl parathion
Development of a one-step microwave-assisted extraction method for simultaneous determination of organophosphorus pesticides and fungicides in soils by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry
Pesticide residues in cashew apple, guava, kaki and peach: GC-μECD, GC-FPD and LC-MS/MS multiresidue method validation, analysis and cumulative acute risk assessment
Molecularly imprinted polymer cartridges coupled to high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC-UV) for simple and rapid analysis of fenthion in olive oil
Electrochemical detection of dual exposure biomarkers of organophosphorus agents based on reactivation of inhibited cholinesterase
Rapid and sensitive detection of methyl-parathion pesticide with an electropolymerized, molecularly imprinted polymer capacitive sensor
Stripping voltammetric analysis of organophosphate pesticides using ni/al layered double hydroxides as solid-phase extraction
Stripping voltammetric analysis of organophosphate pesticides based on solid-phase extraction at zirconia nanoparticles modified electrode
Colorimetric detection of warfare gases by polydiacetylenes toward equipment-free detection
A fluorescent dipyrrinone oxime for the detection of pesticides and other organophosphates
Novel chemiluminescent detection of chemical warfare simulant
A facile microwave avenue to electrochemiluminescent two-color graphene quantum dots
Synthesis of reduced graphene oxide–Fe3O4 multifunctional freestanding membranes and their temperature dependent electronic transport properties
Voltammetric study of pyridine-2-aldoxime methochloride at poly(p-toluene sulfonic acid) modified glassy carbon sensor and its analytical applications
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
A novel protocol for ultra-trace detection of pesticides: combined electrochemical reduction of Ellman’s reagent with acetylcholinesterase inhibition
